title: Fruit scones
subtitle: Classic British scones with sultanas, served with clotted cream and jam
excerpt: A standard self-raising flour scone with sultanas added to the dough. The dough is mixed quickly, barely kneaded, and cut straight from the round without re-rolling the offcuts if possible. High oven heat and gentle dough handling are the two things that matter most. Serve warm with clotted cream and strawberry jam.
sourceNotes: Scones in their current form are documented in British and Scottish cookbooks from at least the early 19th century. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845, Project Gutenberg), gives a recipe for scones using self-rising flour (the precursor to the modern self-raising flour standardised in the UK from the 1840s). Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), includes a plain scone recipe with butter, flour, and milk. The fruit scone with sultanas or currants and the serving custom of clotted cream and jam became standard through the Edwardian cream tea tradition associated with Devon and Cornwall.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked scones on the day of baking. Defrost at room temperature and warm in a 160°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Scones are best eaten on the day they are baked. The dough can be cut and frozen unbaked, then baked from frozen at 220°C for 18 to 20 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 200°C. The high temperature is what gives scones their rise and golden top. The inside should be just cooked through at 12 to 14 minutes.

recipeTools.notes: one baking tray, lined with baking paper

glossaryTerms.definition: The principle that scone dough must be handled as little as possible after the wet ingredients are added to the dry. Overworking develops the gluten, making the finished scone tough, flat, and dense rather than light and risen. The dough should be brought together in 10 to 15 strokes — still slightly rough-looking is fine. When cutting, use a sharp cutter pressed straight down without twisting: twisting seals the edge and prevents the scone rising evenly.

content.text: . Mix the dough until it just comes together, no further. Cut and bake immediately; unlike most pastry doughs, scones should not rest before baking. Use a straight downward cut with the cutter, never twist. Have the oven fully preheated before the dough is made.

Heat the oven to 220°C conventional (200°C fan) and line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift the flour, baking powder, and salt into a large bowl. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Stir in the sugar and sultanas. Beat the egg with the buttermilk. Pour the liquid into the flour mixture and stir with a round-bladed knife until the dough just comes together. It should be slightly sticky.

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and gently press out to 2.5 cm thickness; do not roll. Cut into rounds using a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Gather the trimmings, press together once, and cut any remaining scones. Arrange on the tray and brush the tops with a little beaten egg or milk. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until risen and golden. Transfer to a rack and leave for 5 minutes before splitting. Serve with clotted cream and jam.
